questions.prompt: A call option's payoff at expiration is:
questions.explanation: A call holder exercises if S_T > K, gaining S_T - K. Otherwise lets it expire for $0. Hence max(S_T - K, 0).

questions.prompt: Given: S_0 = $100, K = $100, T = 1 year, r_f = 5%, Call price C = $8. Using put-call parity, what should the put price P be? Answer in dollars to two decimals.
Which of the following are true of an option's TIME VALUE (price minus intrinsic value)?
choices: Largest when the option is at-the-money
questions.explanation: Time value is always non-negative, peaks at-the-money where uncertainty matters most, and decays toward zero at expiration. Even ITM options have non-negative time value.

questions.prompt: Binomial: S_0=$50, u=1.2, d=0.9, K=$50, r_f=4%. Compute the replicating-portfolio delta Δ = (C_u - C_d)/(S_u - S_d).

furtherReading.title: What is Put-Call Parity? Put-Call Parity Formula Explained
furtherReading.why: Clean derivation of put-call parity from no-arbitrage with a binomial-pricing tie-in — both core option ideas in one explainer.
